How do interfaces let single-inheritance languages gain flexibility without the diamond problem?
What is an abstract class, and why can it not be instantiated?
Why are shallow hierarchies generally preferred over deep ones, and what test decides whether a subclass belongs in a hierarchy?

title: Advantages and disadvantages of inheritance in program development
outline: The advantages: reuse, polymorphism, extensibility and clear modelling.
The disadvantages: tight coupling and the fragile base class problem.
Inheritance breaking encapsulation and creating rigid hierarchies.
Composition as an alternative and the guideline to favour it.
Practical criteria for deciding when to use inheritance.
body: Inheritance is a powerful feature, but like every powerful tool it brings both real benefits and real dangers. A competent developer must be able to weigh them, because the decision to use inheritance is one of the most consequential in object-oriented design, shaping how a system can grow and how safely it can be changed for years afterwards. This lesson examines both sides so that the choice can be made deliberately.

The first and most obvious advantage is code reuse. Members common to a family of types are written once in a base class and inherited by all descendants, eliminating duplication and its attendant risk that copies drift out of step. When a shared behaviour must be corrected, fixing it in the base class corrects it everywhere at once, which is far safer than editing many near-identical copies scattered through the codebase.

The second advantage is polymorphism. Because a descendant is a kind of its ancestor, code written against the ancestor type automatically works with every descendant, and virtual dispatch selects the right behaviour at run time. This is what makes systems extensible: new subtypes can be introduced and existing code will use them without modification, satisfying the open-closed principle and letting a program grow gracefully.

The third advantage is clear modelling. When a problem genuinely contains an 'is-a' hierarchy, such as different kinds of accounts or shapes or documents, inheritance lets the code mirror that structure directly, so the design reads like the domain it represents. A hierarchy that reflects real relationships makes the program easier to understand for anyone who knows the problem area.

Against these benefits stand serious disadvantages, the first being tight coupling between ancestor and descendant. A subclass depends not only on its parent's public interface but often on its internal behaviour, so the two are bound together far more tightly than two collaborating but independent classes. This coupling is permanent and cannot be reconfigured at run time, which limits flexibility.

This coupling leads directly to the fragile base class problem. A seemingly harmless change to a base class, such as altering the order in which it calls its own methods, can silently break subclasses that relied on the old behaviour, even though those subclasses were not touched. In a large hierarchy the author of a base class cannot easily know how every descendant depends on it, so changes become risky and are made timidly, which slows the system's evolution.

Inheritance can also weaken encapsulation. To let subclasses build on it, a base class often exposes protected members, revealing more of its internals than a purely public interface would. Descendants then depend on those exposed details, so the base class loses the freedom to change them. In effect, inheritance opens a second, wider interface aimed at subclasses, and that interface is easy to leak too much through.

A further disadvantage is rigidity. A class's place in a hierarchy is fixed at compile time and a single-inheritance language allows only one parent, so if an object needs to combine capabilities from two unrelated hierarchies, inheritance cannot express it directly. Deep hierarchies compound the problem, because the behaviour of a leaf class is scattered across many ancestors and becomes hard to trace and to change.

Misusing inheritance to share incidental code is a common and damaging error. When a class inherits from another merely because it happens to contain useful code, rather than because it is genuinely a kind of that class, the 'is-a' relationship is false. Such misuse violates the Liskov substitution principle, produces surprising behaviour when the subclass is used where the base is expected, and creates coupling with no conceptual justification.

The principal alternative is composition, in which a class holds instances of other classes as fields and delegates work to them, expressing a 'has-a' relationship. Composition avoids the fragile base class problem because the enclosing class depends only on the public interfaces of its parts, and it is more flexible because the parts can be swapped, even at run time, and combined freely from any source. This is why the well-known guideline advises developers to favour composition over inheritance.

Composition is not free of cost either: it can require more code, since the enclosing class must forward calls to its parts, and it does not by itself provide the automatic subtype polymorphism that inheritance gives. The modern answer is to combine composition with interfaces: a class implements an interface for polymorphism and composes helper objects for reuse, gaining flexibility and substitutability without the tight coupling of implementation inheritance.

In practice a developer can apply clear criteria. Use inheritance only when there is a true, stable 'is-a' relationship, when the subclass can substitute for the base everywhere without surprises, and when the base class was designed to be extended. Prefer composition when the relationship is 'has-a' or 'is-implemented-using', when behaviour must be combined from several sources, or when flexibility to change collaborators at run time is valuable. Applying these tests turns the choice from habit into engineering.

Thus, inheritance offers genuine advantages of reuse, polymorphism and faithful modelling, but it carries the costs of tight coupling, fragile base classes, weakened encapsulation and rigidity; a skilled developer uses it only for true, stable 'is-a' relationships and otherwise favours composition with interfaces, choosing deliberately rather than by reflex.
questions: What are the main advantages of inheritance for reuse, polymorphism and modelling?
What is the fragile base class problem, and how does tight coupling between parent and child cause it?
In what way can inheritance weaken the encapsulation of a base class?
Why does misusing inheritance to share incidental code violate the Liskov substitution principle?
What criteria help a developer decide between inheritance and composition for a given relationship?
